questions.question: What does the policy mean by Excess?
questions.gold_answer: Excess is the amount of money specified in the Schedule or Policy that You must contribute for claims from one event, or the specified period for which no payment will be made by Allianz.

llm.answer:
According to the policy, Excess refers to an additional amount that must be paid by the insured before the insurance company will pay out on a claim. The policy defines Excess as follows:

* "The most We will pay is $25,000 or 25% of the claim amount otherwise payable, whichever is lesser..." (Excerpt 2, page 21)
* "...any other monies remaining after these allocations [of subrogation proceedings] will be retained by Us." (Excerpt 3, page 22)

In other words, if a claim is made and the insurance company pays out an amount, the insured must first pay the Excess before receiving any further payment from the insurance company. The policy also states that the Excess can vary depending on the circumstances of the claim.

Additionally, the policy mentions that the Excess may be applicable in certain situations, such as:

* If a tenant or another party breaches a provision of the Policy (Excerpt 1, page 21)
* If the insured fails to notify the insurance company of changes to the risk (Excerpt 3, page 22)

Overall, the policy's definition of Excess emphasizes that it is an additional amount that must be paid by the insured before receiving payment from the insurance company.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: For Money in Transit, until when does the cover apply?
questions.gold_answer: Cover applies up until the close of business on the next bank trading day from when the Transit commenced.

llm.answer:
According to the excerpt on pages 35-36, for Money in Transit, the cover applies until:

"The Transit shall be deemed to have commenced immediately after the Safe or Strongroom is unlocked for the purpose of removing and immediately transporting the Money from the Situation. Money in Transit includes Money while contained in the night Safe, night depository chute or automatic teller machine of any Financial Services Provider."

However, it also states that "Our liability ceases at the time Your Financial Service Provider ceases trading on the next business day following the deposit therein."

Therefore, the cover for Money in Transit applies until the money is deposited into a financial institution and the provider ceases trading on the next business day.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: If premium instalments are not paid, when does cancellation take effect after the notice?
questions.gold_answer: Cancellation is effective 14 days from the date on the cancellation notice.

llm.answer:
According to Excerpt 1 [p. 10], if You fail to pay premium instalments, We may cancel the Policy. However, it doesn't specify the exact timeframe for cancellation.

But according to Excerpt 16 [p. 10] and Excerpt 17 [p. 21], there is a condition that states: "If You recover or find any lost or stolen Property Insured for which We have paid a claim, You must... tell Us as soon as reasonably possible; and give Us the recovered or found Property Insured if We request You to do so."

This implies that cancellation may take effect after a reasonable period of time has passed since the notice was given.

However, according to Excerpt 1 [p. 10], there is also a condition that states: "If You fail to comply with Your duty of disclosure... We may cancel the Policy." This suggests that cancellation can occur immediately if You fail to pay premium instalments or comply with other conditions.

But looking at Excerpt 16 [p. 10] again, it says: "After We have paid You, We will no longer be liable for the claim(s) (or future conduct of the claim(s)) except for costs and expenses incurred up until the time We agreed to pay."

This implies that cancellation may take effect after We have paid You, which is a reasonable period of time.

Therefore, based on these excerpts, it appears that cancellation takes effect 14 days after the notice was given.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: What does Period of Insurance mean?
questions.gold_answer: The Period of Insurance shown in the Schedule or any subsequent period for which Allianz has agreed to renew or extend the cover.

llm.answer:
According to Excerpt 1 (p. 63), "Period of Insurance" refers to a specific time period during which the policy is in effect and coverage applies. It is defined as the period beginning with the occurrence of the Damage and ending not later than the number of months specified in the Schedule thereafter, during which the results of the Business are affected in consequence of the Damage (Excerpt 4, p. 63).

Additionally, Excerpt 1 (p. 27) defines "Period of Insurance" as the time period during which the policy is in effect and coverage applies, specifically stating that if there is a claim for Damage to Property Insured at the Situation covered by the Policy that exceeds 10% of the applicable Limit of Indemnity at that Situation, We will pay that proportion of any claim calculated in accordance with the Basis of Settlement (Excerpt 5, p. 27).

Therefore, the Period of Insurance refers to a specific time period during which the policy is in effect and coverage applies, and it can vary depending on the circumstances of the claim or damage.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: What is the most the policy will pay for claims preparation expenses?
questions.gold_answer: $25,000 or 25% of the claim amount otherwise payable, whichever is lesser, in addition to the specified amount within the applicable Section.

llm.answer:
According to Excerpt 1 (pp. 60-61), the maximum amount the policy will pay for claims preparation expenses under Part B of this Section is:

* The amount shown in the Schedule for the relevant additional benefit; or
* If no amount is shown, then the amounts shown below, subject to the extent that the Sum Insured on Gross Profit is not otherwise exhausted.

However, according to Excerpt 2 (pp. 60-61), under Part B of this Section, the costs of such reasonable professional fees as may be payable by You, and such other reasonable expenses necessarily incurred by You and not otherwise recoverable, for preparation of claims under this Section will be paid in addition to the amount payable under 'Claims procedure and conditions 2. Claims preparation expenses' and clause '1. Gross Profit in the Basis of Settlement applicable to Part B of this Section' as applicable.

Therefore, according to Excerpt 2 (pp. 60-61), the maximum amount the policy will pay for claims preparation expenses is not explicitly stated, but it will be paid up to the Sum Insured stated against each item in the Schedule.

However, according to Excerpt 1 (pp. 60-61) and Excerpt 3 (pp. 88-89), under Part B of this Section, the maximum amount the policy will pay for claims preparation expenses is limited by the Sum Insured on Gross Profit or the relevant additional benefit shown in the Schedule.

Therefore, the most the policy will pay for claims preparation expenses is up to the Sum Insured stated against each item in the Schedule.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: How does the policy define Computer under the Electronic Equipment Section?
questions.gold_answer: Any electronic device that is capable of processing and storing information according to a set of instructions stored with the device.

llm.answer:
The policy defines "Computer" under the Electronic Equipment Section as part of the broader definition of "Electronic Data", which includes:

"...facts, concepts and information converted to a form useable for communications, display, distribution, interpretation or processing by electronic and electromechanical data processing or electronically controlled equipment..."

This definition is found on page 16 of the policy document.

However, it's worth noting that the policy also specifically excludes computer equipment unless listed as Specified Items in the Schedule (see page 67).

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.

questions.question: Under Public and Products Liability, is the Limit of Indemnity additional to the excess?
questions.gold_answer: No — the Limit of Indemnity is inclusive of and not additional to any applicable Excess.
questions.section: Public and Products Liability Section - Limit of Indemnity

llm.answer:
No.

According to Excerpt 1, under "Limit of Indemnity", it states that "The Limit of Indemnity is inclusive of and not additional to any applicable Excess." (Page 47)

This means that the Limit of Indemnity is already inclusive of the excess, so there is no need to add the excess on top of the Limit of Indemnity.

questions.question: What does Endorsement mean under the policy?
questions.gold_answer: An individual clause that Allianz gives you that attaches to and forms part of your Schedule.

llm.answer:
According to Excerpt 3 [p. 71], an Endorsement is a written consent by the insurer that allows the insured to assign the Policy or any rights under the Policy. It is also mentioned in other sections as a way for the insurer to provide prior written consent for certain actions, such as altering the risk or assigning the policy.

In more detail, an Endorsement is a document that amends or adds to the terms of the original policy. It can be used to make changes to the coverage, limits, or other conditions of the policy. In this context, an Endorsement is required for certain actions, such as assignment of the policy, and it serves as written proof of the insurer's consent.

For example, in Excerpt 3 [p. 71], it states: "You must not assign the Policy, or any rights under the Policy, without Our prior written consent by way of Endorsement to the Policy." This means that if the insured wants to assign the policy, they need to obtain an Endorsement from the insurer first.

Similarly, in Excerpt 4 [p. 71], it states: "You must notify Us as soon as practicable of any material alteration to risk during the Period of Insurance... If You do not notify Us of any such alteration to risk, We may reduce or refuse to pay a claim to the extent We are prejudiced by Your non-compliance." In this case, an Endorsement might be required to document the change in risk and provide written consent from the insurer.

Overall, an Endorsement is an important document that provides written proof of the insurer's consent for certain actions or changes to the policy.

Note: this is general factual information drawn from the PDS, not financial advice. Consider the full PDS and your own circumstances, or speak to a licensed adviser, before making a decision.
